title: Smiths Medical CADD Legacy – Battery Won’t Hold Charge / Won’t Power On
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Smiths Medical CADD Legacy infusion pump that will not power on or shows battery failure symptoms.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump is not currently connected to a patient. If the device was in use, transfer therapy to a backup pump before troubleshooting. Disconnect the pump from patient tubing and accessories if necessary.

steps.title: Verify Battery Type and Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump uses two fresh AA alkaline batteries. Replace with new, known-good batteries.

steps.title: Check Battery Orientation and Contacts
steps.instructions: Remove batteries and re-insert, ensuring correct polarity (+/-). Inspect battery compartment for corrosion, dirt, or bent contacts. Clean contacts with a soft cloth or isopropyl alcohol if needed.

steps.title: Attempt Power-On With Fresh Batteries
steps.instructions: Insert new batteries and press the power button. Observe if the pump powers on normally.

steps.title: Observe LED Indicators or Error Messages
steps.instructions: Check for flashing lights or battery warnings.

steps.title: Reset Pump Electronics
steps.instructions: Remove batteries for at least 30 seconds, then reinsert. Attempt power-on again.

CCR.Complaint: Pump will not power on; batteries appear dead.
CCR.Cause: Two AA batteries replaced with fresh alkalines; correct polarity and contacts verified; pump still fails to power on.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service and sent to Biomedical Engineering for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: LED indicators: flashing red or no response
Pump final status: Out of Service

title: Smiths Medical CADD Legacy - Pump Programming Errors / Incorrect Infusion Rates
description: Troubleshooting guide for CADD Legacy infusion pumps that appear to deliver incorrect infusion rates or reject programmed values.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump is not currently connected to a patient before beginning troubleshooting. If infusion accuracy is in question, move the patient to another verified infusion pump. This prevents risk of incorrect medication delivery while the device is being evaluated.

steps.title: Verify the reported issue with the clinical user
steps.instructions: Ask the clinician what specifically occurred. Determine whether the pump delivered too quickly, too slowly, or would not accept the programmed values. Understanding the complaint helps determine whether the issue is programming, setup, or device behavior.

steps.title: Check the infusion mode selected on the pump
steps.instructions: The CADD Legacy supports multiple infusion modes such as continuous infusion, PCA, or intermittent programs depending on configuration. Incorrect mode selection can cause infusion behavior that appears incorrect.

steps.title: Verify programmed parameters
steps.instructions: Review the current settings including infusion rate, dose amount, reservoir volume, and lockout settings if PCA mode is used. Ensure the values entered match the physician’s order or intended programming.

steps.title: Confirm the correct cassette or medication reservoir is installed
steps.instructions: Incorrect cassette types or improperly seated medication reservoirs can cause the pump to behave unexpectedly or restrict programming. Verify the cassette is properly installed and compatible with the pump configuration.

steps.title: Power cycle the pump
steps.instructions: Remove the batteries and wait several seconds. Reinstall the batteries and power the pump back on. This can clear minor software glitches that may affect programming behavior.

steps.title: Test programming with a known standard configuration
steps.instructions: Program a simple test infusion such as a low continuous rate using a test cassette or empty reservoir. Verify that the pump accepts the programmed values, confirm the display shows the correct rate, and verify the pump begins operation normally.

steps.title: Inspect the keypad for proper operation
steps.instructions: If certain numbers or keys do not respond correctly, programming errors may occur due to keypad failure. Press each button and verify that the correct input appears on the display.

steps.title: Check for error messages or alerts
steps.instructions: Review the screen for system alerts such as cassette errors, occlusion alarms, or programming restrictions. These alerts can interfere with infusion setup or operation.

steps.title: Verify battery condition
steps.instructions: Weak batteries can sometimes cause erratic behavior or display issues. Install fresh AA alkaline batteries and test programming again.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported that the CADD Legacy infusion pump appeared to deliver medication faster than the programmed rate and programming values did not match expected infusion behavior.
CCR.Cause: During inspection, pump programming was verified and keypad testing revealed intermittent input errors when entering numerical values.
CCR.Resolution: Pump was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair due to suspected keypad malfunction.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested if external power accessories were used
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Smiths Medical CADD Legacy – Keypad / Buttons Unresponsive
description: Guide for Clinical Engineering to troubleshoot unresponsive keypad/buttons on a Smiths Medical CADD Legacy infusion pump.

steps.title: Ensure Device is Powered
steps.instructions: Verify the pump is connected to a charged battery or AC power. Observe any indicator lights or display startup sequence. Why: A completely unpowered device can appear unresponsive.

steps.title: Inspect for Obstructions or Debris
steps.instructions: Check the keypad for dirt, liquids, or sticky residue. Gently clean with a soft, lint-free cloth and approved cleaning solution. Why: Physical obstruction can prevent button actuation.

steps.title: Check for Keypad Lock or Security Mode
steps.instructions: Some CADD Legacy pumps have keypad lock or programming lock features. Confirm the lock is not engaged. Refer to pump label or display prompts. Why: A locked keypad will not respond to input even if the device is functional.

steps.title: Restart / Power Cycle the Pump
steps.instructions: Turn off the pump, wait 30 seconds, then power back on. Observe any boot-up errors or abnormal behavior. Why: Temporary software glitches can cause the keypad to become unresponsive.

steps.title: Verify Battery Condition
steps.instructions: If the pump runs on battery, remove and reinstall the battery. Test with a known good battery if available. Why: Low voltage may prevent keypad scanning circuits from functioning correctly.

steps.title: Check for Visible Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the keypad for cracked or dislodged buttons. Inspect connectors at the keypad interface if externally accessible. Why: Physical damage or loose connections can interrupt button signals.

steps.title: Test External Controls if Applicable
steps.instructions: If the pump supports external start/stop buttons or remote control, verify if those inputs work. Why: Confirms whether the issue is isolated to the keypad or systemic.

CCR.Complaint: Pump keypad unresponsive; cannot start or adjust infusion.
CCR.Cause: During troubleshooting, pump powered on, battery checked, keypad cleaned; no response from buttons.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for repair.

title: Smiths Medical CADD Legacy – Cassette Not Seated / “Check Cassette” Error
description: Guide for Clinical Engineering to troubleshoot a CADD Legacy infusion pump displaying a 'Check Cassette' error, focusing on external cassette seating and latch checks.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Pump
steps.instructions: Turn the pump off, wait ~10 seconds, then power it back on. Clears temporary sensor misreads and ensures error isn’t a transient fault.

steps.title: Inspect the Cassette and Slot
steps.instructions: Remove the infusion cassette completely. Check for obstructions, debris, or misaligned tubing. Even minor blockage can prevent proper seating.

steps.title: Reinsert the Cassette Properly
steps.instructions: Align the cassette with the pump guides. Insert fully until the latch clicks or stops moving. Proper engagement is required for the sensor to detect the cassette.

steps.title: Check the Latch Mechanism
steps.instructions: Observe that the latch moves freely and is not bent, broken, or sticky. Clean lightly if necessary with a lint-free cloth. Latch interference can prevent sensor detection.

steps.title: Verify Sensor Engagement
steps.instructions: With cassette seated, check if the 'Check Cassette' error clears. If equipped, inspect the sensor visually for damage or misalignment.

steps.title: Attempt Infusion Only After Error Clears
steps.instructions: Only start infusion when the error has cleared and the pump accepts programming.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displays “Check Cassette” error and will not start infusion.
CCR.Cause: Cassette partially seated or latch obstructed by debris.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared debris, reinserted cassette fully, verified latch and sensor engagement, pump operational.

helpfulDetails: Final pump status: operational / out of service if unresolved

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 3500 - Infusion Programming or Rate Errors
description: Guide for Clinical Engineering to troubleshoot infusion programming errors, unexpected rates, or related alarms on Medfusion 3500 syringe pumps.

steps.title: Verify User Programming
steps.instructions: Confirm that the rate, volume, and infusion type entered match the physician’s orders. Check for decimal placement errors or unit mismatches (mL/hr vs. µL/min).

steps.title: Check Syringe Compatibility
steps.instructions: Ensure the syringe size and manufacturer are compatible with the Medfusion 3500. Confirm the syringe is seated correctly in the cassette/holder.

steps.title: Inspect Cassette and Door Sensors
steps.instructions: Verify that the cassette is properly latched and that the pump detects it. Look for debris, wear, or damage on the sensor or cassette interface.

steps.title: Check for Active Alarms or Error Messages
steps.instructions: Note any on-screen errors and verify against the pump’s error reference. Clear non-critical alarms and attempt to reprogram.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Pump
steps.instructions: Remove the pump from service temporarily, turn it off, and then back on. Reprogram a test infusion with a compatible syringe to verify correct behavior.

steps.title: Verify Pump Firmware / Software Version
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump is running the latest approved firmware for your facility. Check for any software update notices from Smiths Medical.

CCR.Complaint: Staff report that the Medfusion 3500 delivered an incorrect infusion rate or programming error.
CCR.Cause: Initial inspection showed correct syringe and cassette placement. Programming errors appear persistent. No user entry errors detected; pump resets did not resolve the issue.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled “Out of Service,” and sent to Biomedical Engineering bench for evaluation. Patient switched to alternate verified infusion pump.

helpfulDetails: Verify syringe type and lot number
Record last service date and battery status

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 3500 - Keypad Unresponsive
description: Guide to troubleshoot unresponsive keypad issues on a Medfusion 3500 syringe infusion pump, ruling out lockout, contamination, or system freezes before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Confirm the Pump Is Powered and Fully Booted
steps.instructions: Verify the pump is powered on and the display is active. Check that the screen shows the normal interface. If the pump appears frozen, the keypad may seem unresponsive. If frozen, proceed to the power cycle step.

steps.title: Check for Keypad Lockout Mode
steps.instructions: The pump may be placed in a locked state during clinical use. Look for a lock icon or message indicating controls are locked. Attempt the standard unlock sequence used by your facility. A locked keypad will appear nonfunctional even though hardware is fine.

steps.title: Inspect the Keypad Surface
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the keypad for residue from cleaning wipes or medications, sticky keys or debris between buttons, and physical damage to the keys. Clean any contamination with an approved hospital disinfectant wipe and dry thoroughly.

steps.title: Check for Physical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Verify that no external items interfere with button operation (misaligned labels, tape, debris). Remove any obstruction and retest.

steps.title: Perform a Full Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn the pump off. Disconnect AC power. Wait ~30 seconds. Reconnect power and restart the pump. This can reset the internal control system and resolve temporary freezes.

steps.title: Test All Keys
steps.instructions: After restarting, press each key individually. Note if certain keys respond while others do not. Partial response may indicate failing keypad contacts.

steps.title: Verify No Active Alarm Blocks Input
steps.instructions: Some alarms or prompts require acknowledgment before other keys function. Check for alarm messages or confirmation prompts. Clear any pending prompts and retest.

steps.title: Check for Signs of Internal Damage
steps.instructions: If keys remain unresponsive, look for signs of wear, damage, or repeated malfunction. This may indicate an internal keypad interface failure.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported the Medfusion 3500 keypad would not respond during programming.
CCR.Cause: During testing, keypad buttons did not register input after cleaning and power cycling.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service and sent to Clinical Engineering for evaluation and repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (in service or removed)

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 3500 – Door or Cassette Not Detected
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Medfusion 3500 infusion pump reporting a door open or cassette detection alarm.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting, confirm the pump is not actively infusing on a patient. If it is in use, move the patient to another pump before continuing troubleshooting.

steps.title: Open and Fully Re-Close the Syringe Door
steps.instructions: Open the pump door completely and close it again firmly. Check for door fully seated, audible or tactile latch engagement, and door alignment with the pump body. The Medfusion 3500 uses a door sensor that must detect a fully closed latch. If the door is slightly misaligned, the sensor may not register it as closed.

steps.title: Verify Correct Syringe Installation
steps.instructions: Remove the syringe and reinstall it carefully. Confirm the syringe barrel is seated correctly in the holder, the plunger is aligned with the pump driver mechanism, and the syringe size matches the programmed syringe type. Improperly seated syringes can prevent the door from closing completely or interfere with cassette detection.

steps.title: Inspect the Door Latch Area
steps.instructions: Look closely at the latch and surrounding area. Check for cracked or loose latch components, bent plastic around the door hinge, and foreign objects preventing full closure. Even minor mechanical damage can prevent the door switch from activating.

steps.title: Check the Door Sensor Area
steps.instructions: Inspect the area where the door meets the pump frame. Look for dirt or dried medication residue, tape or labels interfering with closure, or obstructions around the door switch area. If contamination is present, clean the area using appropriate approved cleaning methods.

steps.title: Try a Different Syringe
steps.instructions: Install a different compatible syringe and check whether the pump detects the syringe normally and allows the door alarm to clear. Some syringe brands or worn plungers can interfere with proper seating and detection.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Pump
steps.instructions: Turn the pump off and back on. After reboot, install the syringe again, close the door firmly, and observe whether the alarm clears. Occasionally the pump software may need to reinitialize the door detection system.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported the Medfusion 3500 pump would not start infusion and displayed a door or cassette detection alarm.
CCR.Cause: Syringe was not fully seated in the holder, preventing the door from fully engaging the sensor.
CCR.Resolution: Reinstalled syringe correctly and verified door closure. Alarm cleared and pump passed operational check.

helpfulDetails: Whether issue resolved after reseating syringe
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 3500 – Battery Charging Failure / Won’t Power On
description: Troubleshooting guide for Medfusion 3500 infusion pumps that will not power on or fail to charge, focusing on external power verification before internal repair.

steps.title: Verify the AC power source
steps.instructions: Confirm the outlet is functioning by testing with another device or outlet tester. If the outlet is controlled by a wall switch or power strip, ensure it is turned on. Why this matters: The pump cannot charge or power on without a functioning AC source.

steps.title: Inspect the power cord and adapter
steps.instructions: Check the AC power cord and power supply brick for visible damage such as frayed insulation, bent pins, or loose connectors. Ensure the cord is firmly seated in both the outlet and the pump’s power input. If possible, swap the power cord or power supply with a known-good one of the same type.

steps.title: Check the power indicator lights
steps.instructions: Connect the pump to AC power and observe any indicator lights or display activity. Some pumps will briefly illuminate the screen or charging indicator when power is applied. Note any abnormal behavior such as flickering lights or no response at all.

steps.title: Attempt a power cycle
steps.instructions: Disconnect the pump from AC power, wait approximately 10 seconds, then reconnect it and attempt to power it on again using the power button. This can sometimes reset minor power state issues.

steps.title: Inspect the charging connector
steps.instructions: Examine the DC input port on the pump for debris, bent contacts, or looseness. Gently reseat the power connector and ensure it fits securely. A loose or damaged connector can prevent battery charging or AC operation.

steps.title: Allow the pump to charge
steps.instructions: If the pump powers on only while connected to AC power, allow it to remain plugged in for at least 30 minutes and observe whether the battery begins charging. A deeply discharged battery may take time before normal operation resumes.

steps.title: Check for battery-related symptoms
steps.instructions: Signs of battery failure may include: Pump shuts off immediately when unplugged, Battery indicator does not increase during charging, Pump requires AC power to operate. These symptoms suggest internal battery degradation.

CCR.Complaint: Medfusion 3500 infusion pump would not power on and appeared completely dead.
CCR.Cause: Pump did not power on during testing. Verified outlet was functional and power supply was connected. Pump only briefly illuminated when plugged in and shut off immediately when unplugged, indicating battery failure.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for battery replacement and internal power circuit evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Power cord and power supply inspected or swapped
Display activity when connected to AC power
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 3500 - Occlusion or Line Blockage Alarms
description: Troubleshooting guide for occlusion or line blockage alarms on the Smiths Medical Medfusion 3500 syringe infusion pump.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the pump is connected to a patient, coordinate with clinical staff to transition the infusion to another pump before performing troubleshooting. Patient safety always takes priority.

steps.title: Confirm the Alarm Message
steps.instructions: Verify the exact alarm shown on the screen such as Occlusion or Downstream Occlusion. Confirm whether the alarm occurs immediately upon starting infusion or after the pump has been running. Immediate alarms often indicate setup issues, while delayed alarms may indicate gradual pressure buildup.

steps.title: Check for Closed Clamps in the Infusion Line
steps.instructions: Inspect the entire tubing set for any closed roller clamps or slide clamps including the IV tubing clamp, extension set clamp, and clamp near the syringe. Even a partially closed clamp can trigger an occlusion alarm.

steps.title: Inspect the Tubing Path for Kinks or Compression
steps.instructions: Trace the infusion line from the syringe to the patient connection point and look for sharp bends, kinks, tubing caught under equipment or bed rails, or compression from rails or tape. Correct any obstruction and restart the infusion.

steps.title: Check the Syringe Installation
steps.instructions: Confirm the syringe is correctly seated in the pump and secured by the syringe clamp. Verify the correct syringe size selected in the pump settings, ensure the syringe plunger is aligned with the drive mechanism, and check for misalignment or obstruction at the plunger pusher.

steps.title: Verify Correct Syringe Type and Size
steps.instructions: Confirm that the installed syringe matches the syringe type programmed in the pump. Incorrect syringe brand profiles or incorrect syringe sizes can cause inaccurate pressure detection and occlusion alarms.

steps.title: Inspect the Pressure Sensor Area
steps.instructions: Look at the area where the tubing or syringe interacts with the pump pressure sensing components and check for debris, fluid contamination, or improper tubing placement. Do not attempt internal disassembly.

steps.title: Replace the Tubing Set or Syringe
steps.instructions: If external restrictions are not identified, replace the tubing set or syringe with a new sterile set. Tubing defects or partially occluded filters can cause repeated alarms.

steps.title: Restart the Pump and Observe Operation
steps.instructions: After correcting or replacing components restart the infusion and monitor for repeated occlusion alarms. Observe the pump during initial flow to confirm normal operation.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported Medfusion 3500 pump repeatedly alarming for Occlusion during infusion.
CCR.Cause: Inspection revealed IV tubing kinked under patient bed rail causing downstream flow restriction.
CCR.Resolution: Tubing repositioned and infusion restarted. Pump tested and operated normally without further occlusion alarms.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or removed)

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 - Syringe Recognition or Calibration Errors
description: Troubleshooting syringe recognition or calibration errors on the Medfusion 4000 infusion pump, focusing on external causes before internal failure.

steps.title: Verify Power and Start-Up
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump is powered on and battery is charged or unit is connected to AC. Confirm the pump completes its startup self-test without error. Power fluctuations can prevent proper sensor initialization.

steps.title: Inspect Syringe Type and Compatibility
steps.instructions: Verify the syringe is a compatible model per manufacturer guidelines. Check for expiration or damaged syringes. The pump may reject incompatible syringes or fail calibration.

steps.title: Check Syringe Loading
steps.instructions: Ensure syringe is seated correctly in the pump mechanism. Confirm that the plunger and barrel align with pump sensors. Misalignment can prevent recognition or cause calibration failure.

steps.title: Clean Syringe and Sensor Contacts
steps.instructions: Remove the syringe and inspect for residue, blood, or fluid contamination. Wipe sensor areas with approved cleaning solution. Dirt or residue can interfere with mechanical or optical sensors.

steps.title: Cycle Power and Retry Recognition
steps.instructions: Power the pump off, remove the syringe, wait 30 seconds, then reinsert syringe and power on. Observe whether the pump recognizes the syringe. Simple resets often clear minor sensor or software glitches.

steps.title: Verify Calibration Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump software is set for the syringe size in use. Run any recommended calibration routine per manufacturer instructions (external checks only). Incorrect software settings can trigger recognition or calibration errors.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displays “Syringe Not Recognized” or fails calibration with every attempt.
CCR.Cause: Syringe was misaligned; sensor contacts contaminated.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned sensor contacts, properly seated syringe, and verified calibration routine. Therapy successfully initiated.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual sounds during recognition attempt

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 - Premature Occlusion Alarm
description: Guide for Clinical Engineering to troubleshoot premature occlusion alarms on the Medfusion 4000 infusion pump, focusing on external causes before internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Verify the patient is not actively receiving therapy during troubleshooting. Move the patient to another functional pump if needed.

steps.title: Check Tubing and Syringe
steps.instructions: Inspect the tubing for kinks, bends, or compression from clamps. Confirm the syringe is compatible with the Medfusion 4000 and correctly seated in the pump carriage. Ensure tubing is properly connected to the syringe tip and infusion site.

steps.title: Verify Pump Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct syringe size is selected on the pump’s programming screen. Check for any residual air bubbles in the line that may trigger occlusion detection. Make sure the line has sufficient priming and is free-flowing.

steps.title: Inspect the Pump Mechanism
steps.instructions: Examine the plunger drive and carriage for obstruction or debris. Ensure the plunger holder engages the syringe properly and moves smoothly.

steps.title: Test External Power and Battery
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump is connected to a reliable power source or fully charged battery. Verify no low-battery warnings are present that may affect sensor readings.

steps.title: Run a Self-Test / Calibration
steps.instructions: Perform the pump’s built-in occlusion and flow tests if available. Observe if alarms persist during test mode without patient connection.

CCR.Complaint: Pump triggers occlusion alarm immediately after starting infusion.
CCR.Cause: Observed tubing slightly kinked at patient connection; syringe misaligned in carriage.
CCR.Resolution: Straightened tubing, reseated syringe, verified correct syringe size selection, pump tested successfully.

helpfulDetails: No unusual sounds, heat, or smells noted

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 - Keypad or Touchscreen Input Failure
description: Guide to troubleshoot keypad or touchscreen input failures on the Medfusion 4000 infusion pump, prioritizing external causes before internal repair.

steps.title: Ensure the Pump is Powered Properly
steps.instructions: Verify AC power is connected and the pump is turned on. Check the battery level; low battery can cause erratic touchscreen behavior.

steps.title: Inspect the Keypad / Touchscreen Surface
steps.instructions: Look for debris, liquid, or adhesive residue on buttons or touchscreen. Clean with a lint-free cloth and manufacturer-approved cleaning solution.

steps.title: Check for Visible Damage
steps.instructions: Examine the keypad or touchscreen for cracks, wear, or signs of delamination.

steps.title: Verify Device Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure the front panel or control board ribbon cable (if accessible without disassembly) is seated properly.

steps.title: Cycle Power and Reset the Device
steps.instructions: Turn the pump off, wait 10–15 seconds, and turn it back on. Observe if the keypad or touchscreen responds after reboot.

steps.title: Test Input with Different Settings
steps.instructions: Try entering multiple simple commands or programming steps to see if any keys or screen areas respond.

CCR.Complaint: Pump touchscreen does not respond; keypad entries fail to register.
CCR.Cause: External inspection revealed no debris or visible damage; device reboot did not resolve issue. Likely internal touchscreen or control board failure.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled “Out of Service,” and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Battery status and AC connection verified.
Any partial input responses or intermittent behavior.

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 - Battery Charging or Runtime Failure
description: Troubleshooting battery charging failures and reduced runtime on the Medfusion 4000 infusion pump.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump is plugged into a working outlet using a verified device or meter. Inspect the AC adapter and cord for damage or loose connections. Why: Faulty outlets or power cords are common causes of charging failure.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure the battery is properly seated in its compartment and that contacts are clean. Remove and reinsert the battery to confirm secure connection. Why: Poor contact can prevent charging or reduce runtime.

steps.title: Check Battery Indicator and Alarm Messages
steps.instructions: Observe the battery icon or on-screen messages for status. Record any error codes or warnings for documentation. Why: Error messages indicate whether the battery is recognized, charging, or defective.

steps.title: Test With Known Good Battery
steps.instructions: If available, replace the pump battery with a fully charged, verified battery. Observe if the pump charges and maintains runtime. Why: Confirms whether the issue lies with the battery or the pump circuitry.

steps.title: Environmental and Usage Considerations
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump has not been exposed to extreme temperatures. Avoid long-term storage with a depleted battery. Why: Lithium-ion batteries lose capacity if stored improperly or exposed to temperature extremes.

steps.title: Check Charger or Charging Dock (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Use a verified charging dock or AC adapter. Confirm charger indicators show proper operation. Why: Charging accessories can fail and mimic battery issues.

CCR.Complaint: Pump battery will not charge and runtime is shorter than expected.
CCR.Cause: Battery failed to hold charge after testing; charger and power supply verified functional.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled, and sent for vendor repair.

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 - Drug Library or Configuration Corruption
description: Troubleshooting guide for issues where the Medfusion 4000 infusion pump fails to access or correctly apply its drug library or configuration settings.

steps.title: Verify Device Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump is powered on and completes its self-test without alarms. Check for error messages related to the drug library or configuration.

steps.title: Check External Media or Network Connection
steps.instructions: If updates are applied via USB, SD card, or network, ensure the media is properly inserted and readable. Inspect for damage, dirt, or loose connections.

steps.title: Confirm Device Software Version
steps.instructions: Navigate to the system info screen and note the software and drug library version. Compare with the hospital’s approved version list.

steps.title: Attempt Library Reload or Configuration Restore
steps.instructions: Use the manufacturer-approved process to reload the drug library or restore default configuration. Ensure the pump is on AC power during this operation.

steps.title: Check Logs for Errors
steps.instructions: Access event logs or error history on the pump. Look for failed update attempts, read/write errors, or repeated library load failures.

CCR.Complaint: Pump shows missing drug profiles and errors when loading therapy.
CCR.Cause: External media checked; pump failed to reload drug library; configuration appears corrupted.
CCR.Resolution: Attempted reload using manufacturer-approved procedure; issue persists. Device labeled Out of Service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: AC power confirmed during reload attempt
Media checked and swapped if applicable
Software and drug library versions noted

title: Smiths Medical Level 1 Series H-1000 / H-1200 Fluid & Blood Warmer - Heater Not Reaching Set Temperature
description: Low or unstable fluid temperature despite heater being on, often caused by setup, flow rate, or accessory-related issues rather than internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove device from active patient use. Switch to an alternate warming method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Power and Unit Status
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is powered on, no active alarms, and the heater is enabled. Expected: Display shows active heating mode and target temperature.

steps.title: Check Set Temperature vs Actual Temperature
steps.instructions: Compare the setpoint with displayed outlet temperature. Why it matters: Confirms whether the heater is attempting to reach the correct value.

steps.title: Inspect Disposable Cassette / Set Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure the cassette is properly seated and fully latched. Expected: No gaps, secure fit, no “cassette not detected” warnings.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Disposable Type
steps.instructions: Verify the correct Level 1 compatible cassette/set is being used. Why it matters: Incorrect disposables can limit heat transfer efficiency.

steps.title: Check Fluid Flow Rate
steps.instructions: Assess current infusion rate. Extremely high flow rates may prevent adequate heating. Action: Reduce flow temporarily and observe temperature response. Expected: Temperature should rise when flow is reduced.

steps.title: Inspect Fluid Source Temperature
steps.instructions: Determine if fluids are starting very cold (e.g., refrigerated blood). Why it matters: The unit may not compensate fast enough at high flow rates.

steps.title: Check for Air in Line or Improper Priming
steps.instructions: Look for air gaps or incomplete priming in the cassette. Expected: Fully primed line with continuous fluid column.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing and Connections
steps.instructions: Ensure no kinks, occlusions, or loose connections restricting flow or heat transfer.

steps.title: Evaluate Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Check if the unit is in a cold OR or exposed to airflow (vents, doors). Why it matters: Ambient conditions can impact heating performance.

steps.title: Perform a Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 30 seconds, and restart. Expected: System resets and resumes normal heating operation.

steps.title: Test with Known Good Setup
steps.instructions: Swap cassette and tubing with a new set. Expected: If resolved, issue was related to disposable or setup.

CCR.Complaint: Fluid warmer not reaching set temperature during transfusion.
CCR.Cause: High flow rate combined with improperly seated cassette reduced effective heat transfer.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated cassette and adjusted flow rate; verified unit reached and maintained set temperature.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested and stable power confirmed
Cassette type and lot number checked
No unusual sounds, heat, or odor
Device passed/failed functional test after intervention
Final device status (returned to service or sent to repair)

title: Smiths Medical Level 1 Series H-1000 / H-1200 Fluid & Blood Warmer - Over-temperature safety shutoff activating
description: Troubleshooting frequent over-temperature alarms and safety shutdowns typically caused by flow, setup, or disposable issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from patient use immediately and transition to an alternate warming method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Alarm Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is displaying an over-temperature or safety shutdown alarm. Expected: Heater disabled with alarm indication.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 10–15 seconds, then power back on. Clears temporary faults or sensor glitches. If alarm clears and does not return, issue is resolved.

steps.title: Check Set Temperature
steps.instructions: Verify the setpoint is within normal operating range (typically ~37–41°C). Excessively high setpoints can trigger safety shutoffs.

steps.title: Inspect Disposable Cassette / Set Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure cassette is fully seated, properly latched, and aligned. Expected: No gaps, no cassette errors. Poor thermal contact can cause localized overheating.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Disposable Type
steps.instructions: Verify the cassette/set is correct for Level 1 use and not expired or damaged. Incorrect or degraded disposables can disrupt heat transfer.

steps.title: Assess Fluid Flow Rate
steps.instructions: Check infusion rate. Extremely low or no flow can cause overheating. Expected: Adequate continuous flow. Adjust rate or confirm upstream infusion source if low.

steps.title: Check for Line Occlusions or Kinks
steps.instructions: Inspect all tubing for clamps, kinks, or blockages. Restricted flow traps heat in the cassette, triggering shutdown.

steps.title: Evaluate Fluid Source and Temperature
steps.instructions: Confirm fluid is not already excessively warm prior to entering the unit. Pre-warmed fluids can push outlet temperature above limits.

steps.title: Inspect Temperature Sensors (External Check Only)
steps.instructions: Ensure no visible damage, contamination, or improper placement of temperature probes within the cassette. Incorrect readings can falsely trigger safety shutdown.

steps.title: Check for Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit is not near external heat sources or vents blowing hot air. Ambient heat can affect sensor readings and system response.

CCR.Complaint: Unit repeatedly shuts down with over-temperature alarm during use in OR.
CCR.Cause: Low flow rate due to partially occluded IV tubing causing localized overheating in cassette.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared occlusion, restored proper flow, and verified normal operation without further alarms.

helpfulDetails: Flow rate at time of alarm

title: Smiths Medical Level 1 Series H-1000 / H-1200 Fluid & Blood Warmer - Cassette Not Detected
description: Troubleshooting cassette detection failures caused by installation issues, damaged disposables, or sensor problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use. Transition to an alternate warming method before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Alarm / Message
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is displaying a “cassette not detected” or similar error. Expected: Unit will not allow heating without cassette recognition.

steps.title: Inspect Cassette Installation
steps.instructions: Open and reseat the cassette. Ensure it is fully inserted and latched. Expected: Cassette sits flush with no gaps. If properly seated and detected → issue resolved.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions or Debris
steps.instructions: Inspect the cassette slot and sensor area for fluid, debris, or packaging remnants. Why it matters: Optical/mechanical sensors can be blocked. Clean if needed and reinsert cassette.

steps.title: Verify Correct Disposable Cassette Type
steps.instructions: Confirm the cassette is a compatible Level 1 set for the H-1000 / H-1200. Why it matters: Incorrect or off-brand disposables may not align with sensors.

steps.title: Inspect Cassette for Damage
steps.instructions: Check for cracks, warped plastic, or damaged alignment features. Expected: Cassette structure intact and rigid. If damaged → replace cassette and retest.

steps.title: Try a New Cassette Set
steps.instructions: Install a new, unopened cassette. Why it matters: Eliminates defective disposable as root cause. If new cassette is detected → original cassette was faulty.

steps.title: Check Cassette Latch / Door Mechanism
steps.instructions: Ensure the latch fully closes and engages. Expected: Firm closure with no looseness. If latch does not fully engage → sensor may not trigger.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 10–15 seconds, then power back on with cassette installed. Why it matters: Clears temporary detection faults. If cassette is now recognized → issue resolved.

steps.title: Observe for Intermittent Detection
steps.instructions: Gently press or slightly adjust cassette while inserted (no force). Why it matters: Intermittent detection may indicate worn latch or sensor alignment issue.

CCR.Complaint: Unit displaying “cassette not detected” and unable to initiate warming.
CCR.Cause: Failed cassette recognition due to defective disposable cassette (or internal sensor failure if unresolved).
CCR.Resolution: Replaced cassette and verified proper detection (or removed from service for repair if issue persisted).

helpfulDetails: Cassette seating and latch function checked
Presence of debris or fluid in slot

title: Smiths Medical Level 1 Series H-1000 / H-1200 Fluid & Blood Warmer - Temperature Sensor Calibration Drift
description: Troubleshooting temperature drift and inaccurate readings on Smiths Medical Level 1 fluid warmers.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use. Transfer to an alternate warming method before troubleshooting. Expected: No patient connected during evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Compare the displayed temperature to expected output or external thermometer if available. Expected: Confirm whether true drift exists vs perception.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, wait 10–15 seconds, then power back on. Why it matters: Clears temporary sensor or software anomalies. If readings return to normal → issue resolved.

steps.title: Check Set Temperature and Mode
steps.instructions: Verify correct temperature setpoint (~37–41°C typical) and proper operating mode. Why it matters: Incorrect settings can appear as drift. If corrected and stable → issue resolved.

steps.title: Inspect Disposable Cassette / Set
steps.instructions: Ensure cassette is fully seated, properly latched, and not damaged. Expected: Secure fit, no leaks or deformities. Why it matters: Poor thermal transfer can mimic sensor drift. If corrected → recheck temperature.

steps.title: Verify Fluid Flow Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm appropriate flow rate (not excessively high or intermittent). Why it matters: High flow reduces heating efficiency and may appear as inaccurate temperature. If adjusted and stable → issue resolved.

steps.title: Check for Air in the Line
steps.instructions: Inspect for air bubbles or incomplete priming. Why it matters: Air reduces heat transfer and affects sensed temperature. Prime system as needed and reassess.

steps.title: Inspect for Environmental Influences
steps.instructions: Check if unit is exposed to cold airflow, OR vents, or drafts. Why it matters: External cooling can cause temperature discrepancies. If repositioned and stabilized → issue resolved.

steps.title: Observe for Consistency
steps.instructions: Monitor whether temperature fluctuation is constant, drifting, or intermittent. Why it matters: Helps distinguish setup-related issues from true sensor instability.

steps.title: Cross-Check with Another Unit (If Available)
steps.instructions: Run the same setup on a known-good unit. Expected: If issue follows setup → external cause. If not → device-specific issue.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported fluid warmer not maintaining accurate temperature; readings appeared lower than expected.
CCR.Cause: Temperature discrepancy persisted after confirming proper setup, flow, and cassette—suspected sensor calibration drift.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service and sent to Biomed shop/vendor for calibration verification and repair.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or lack of alarms

title: Smiths Medical Level 1 Series H-1000 / H-1200 Fluid & Blood Warmer - Power Supply or Heater Plate Failure
description: Device not powering on, intermittent shutdowns, or no heat output despite correct setup, often related to power input, connections, or external factors.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from active patient use and transition to an alternate warming method. Expected: No patient connected during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Check that the unit is plugged into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Test the outlet with another device if needed. Expected: Outlet provides consistent power. If outlet is faulty → move to a working outlet and retest.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Examine the power cord for damage, loose connections, or bent prongs. Ensure it is fully seated in the unit. Why it matters: Damaged cords can mimic internal power failure. If damaged → replace cord and retest.

steps.title: Check Power Switch and Indicators
steps.instructions: Turn the unit on and observe for any lights, display activity, or fan noise. Expected: Normal startup indicators present. If no response → continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Unplug the unit, wait 15–30 seconds, then reconnect and power on. Why it matters: Resets internal protection circuits. If unit powers on normally → issue resolved.

steps.title: Verify Circuit Breaker / Internal Protection (External Check Only)
steps.instructions: If accessible externally, check for a resettable breaker or fuse indicator. Expected: No tripped condition. If reset restores function → monitor for recurrence.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Cassette Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure a compatible Level 1 cassette is fully inserted and latched. Why it matters: Some units will not enable heating without proper cassette detection. If reseating restores heating → issue resolved.

steps.title: Check for Over-Temperature or Prior Alarm History
steps.instructions: Determine if the unit recently shut down due to an alarm condition. Why it matters: Safety shutdowns can disable heating temporarily. If resolved after reset → monitor for repeat faults.

steps.title: Assess Heater Function
steps.instructions: With proper setup, confirm whether the unit attempts to heat (temperature rising or heater indicator active). Expected: Temperature should begin increasing. If powered but no heating occurs → possible heater plate issue.

steps.title: Inspect for Signs of Physical Damage or Overheating
steps.instructions: Check for unusual smells, discoloration, or excessive heat from the housing. Why it matters: Indicates internal component failure. If present → remove from service immediately.

CCR.Complaint: Unit would not power on / no heat output reported by OR staff.
CCR.Cause: Failed internal power supply or heater plate suspected after verifying outlet, cord, and setup.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service, labeled out of service, and sent to Biomed/vendor for repair.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual smell, heat, or noise
Final device status (in service vs removed)

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 Infusion Pump - Alarm: Occlusion - Check Infusion Line
description: Troubleshooting guide for Occlusion - Check Infusion Line alarms caused by downstream restriction, catheter resistance, syringe loading issues, high pressure setup, occlusion configuration, or force sensing problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the pump is actively infusing and an occlusion alarm is present, notify clinical staff immediately. Do not silence, bypass, or repeatedly restart the pump without confirming that the infusion path is safe. Transfer therapy to another verified pump or alternate infusion method if medication delivery is critical.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Occlusion - Check Infusion Line. Note whether the alarm occurs immediately after start, after several minutes, only at higher rates, or only with certain syringes, tubing, fluids, or clinical setups.

steps.title: Check the Infusion Line for Simple External Causes
steps.instructions: Inspect the infusion path from syringe to patient connection. Look for closed clamps, kinked tubing, pinched tubing, tubing caught in bed rails, stopcocks in the wrong position, blocked extension sets, or loose tubing connections.

steps.title: Check the Syringe and Pump Loading
steps.instructions: Verify the syringe barrel is seated correctly, the flange is secured, the plunger is captured, the syringe size and model match the pump selection, and the plunger driver is not binding.

steps.title: Check for Catheter or Patient-Side Resistance
steps.instructions: Have clinical staff assess the patient-side access for infiltration, occluded catheter, restricted IV site, closed access device, resistance when flushing, catheter size concerns, or patient positioning that restricts flow.

steps.title: Review the Infusion Setup, Fluid, and Rate
steps.instructions: Review infusion rate, syringe size, tubing bore size, extension set length, filters, medication viscosity, fluid concentration, catheter size, and total line length. Small tubing, high rates, long extension sets, or viscous fluids can increase pressure.

steps.title: Check Occlusion Alarm Configuration
steps.instructions: Review the configured occlusion pressure or force limit if the alarm appears too sensitive or inconsistent with the clinical setup. Confirm settings match facility policy, medication type, care area, syringe type, infusion profile, or drug library configuration.

steps.title: Consider FlowSentry Pressure Monitoring if Used
steps.instructions: If FlowSentry pressure monitoring is used, verify the pressure sensing setup, pressure tubing or accessory connection, compatible accessory use, displayed pressure trend, and whether pressure rises before the alarm.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Binding or Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Externally inspect for cracked syringe holder components, damaged plunger driver, sticky or contaminated plunger mechanism, fluid intrusion, loose syringe clamp components, recent drop damage, or unusual noise during plunger movement.

steps.title: Check Force Sensor Calibration if the Alarm Recurs
steps.instructions: If actual occlusion, catheter resistance, line setup, syringe loading, and configuration issues have been ruled out, evaluate the force sensor through the approved Biomed process using Biomed > Calibration > Calibrate Force Sensor according to facility procedure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm is corrected by opening a clamp, removing a kink, changing tubing setup, correcting syringe loading, resolving catheter resistance, or completing approved force sensor calibration, verify safe function and return the pump to service only after operational checks pass.

CCR.Complaint: Medfusion 4000 infusion pump reported Occlusion - Check Infusion Line during infusion. User stated pump stopped delivery and alarm returned after restart.
CCR.Cause: Force sensor detected pressure above configured occlusion limit. Infusion line was found kinked near the patient connection, causing downstream restriction.
CCR.Resolution: Removed kink from infusion line, verified syringe was loaded correctly, confirmed alarm cleared, and ran pump without repeated occlusion alarm. Device returned to service.

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 Infusion Pump - Alarm: Check Syringe Barrel Clamp
description: Troubleshooting guide for Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 Check Syringe Barrel Clamp alarms that may prevent syringe loading verification or reliable barrel clamp position detection.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the pump from patient use if the alarm prevents syringe verification or delivery. Transfer the infusion to another verified pump if therapy must continue.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is Check Syringe Barrel Clamp and identify whether it occurs at startup, during syringe loading, before infusion begins, after the clamp is lifted, or intermittently during setup.

steps.title: Confirm the Barrel Clamp Is Actually in Position
steps.instructions: Verify the syringe barrel clamp is fully lowered onto the syringe barrel and is not lifted, partially seated, loose, stuck, or resting on the pump handle.

steps.title: Check for Startup Position Issues
steps.instructions: Determine whether the pump was powered on while the barrel clamp was lifted or resting against the handle. Restart the pump with the clamp in its normal resting position if needed.

steps.title: Verify Syringe Model and Size
steps.instructions: Confirm the syringe model and size are approved for the pump configuration and that the selected syringe on the screen matches the installed syringe.

steps.title: Reload the Syringe Correctly
steps.instructions: Remove and reload the syringe carefully. Confirm the barrel is seated, flange is positioned, plunger is captured, and the barrel clamp lowers smoothly without obstruction.

steps.title: Check for Physical Obstruction or Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the barrel clamp area for cracked parts, residue, contamination, tape, labels, debris, bent components, loose parts, or resistance during clamp movement.

steps.title: Follow the Pump Prompt If Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the pump provides an alarm silence or confirmation sequence and the syringe is verified to be correctly loaded, follow the directed sequence. Do not bypass the alarm if loading is questionable.

steps.title: Test Barrel Clamp Sensor Function in Biomed Diagnostics
steps.instructions: Remove the pump from clinical use if the alarm repeats. Use Biomed > Diagnostics > Monitor Digital Sensors and verify the barrel clamp sensor changes state consistently as the clamp is raised and lowered.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Cause
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is loading-related, startup-position related, syringe compatibility related, or device-related based on alarm behavior and sensor response.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 displaying Check Syringe Barrel Clamp alarm during syringe loading. Pump would not reliably verify barrel clamp position.
CCR.Cause: Barrel clamp position alarm confirmed. Syringe loading, clamp seating, syringe model and size selection, and startup clamp position were checked. Alarm repeated after external loading checks.
CCR.Resolution: Verified syringe was properly seated and barrel clamp was fully lowered. Checked for obstruction or damage at clamp area. Followed pump confirmation sequence when appropriate. Removed pump from service for bench evaluation of barrel clamp sensor using Biomed diagnostics after alarm repeated.

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 Infusion Pump - Alarm: Check Syringe Flange Sensor
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Check Syringe Flange Sensor alarm on the Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 infusion pump, including syringe loading checks, flange holder movement, barrel clamp engagement, diagnostic sensor verification, and escalation guidance.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the pump is connected to a patient and infusion delivery is interrupted or cannot be trusted, remove the pump from patient use. Transfer the infusion to another verified pump if clinically required. Do not continue using the pump if syringe loading, flange position, or sensor confirmation cannot be trusted.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Check Syringe Flange Sensor. Note whether the alarm occurs during syringe loading, at startup, when attempting to begin infusion, intermittently during use, or after the barrel clamp or syringe was moved. Identify associated symptoms such as inability to start infusion, syringe not recognized, repeated loading prompts, or alarm recurrence after reloading.

steps.title: Remove and Reload the Syringe
steps.instructions: Remove the syringe and reload it carefully. Confirm the syringe barrel is seated correctly in the barrel clamp and the syringe flange is properly positioned in the flange holder. If the alarm clears after proper reloading, verify normal operation before returning the pump to service.

steps.title: Check the Syringe Flange Holder
steps.instructions: Inspect the flange holder for obstruction, damage, stiffness, contamination, or anything preventing full travel. Confirm the flange holder pulls back toward the side of the case and returns freely. Look for sticky residue, dried medication, cracked plastic, or debris around the holder path.

steps.title: Confirm Barrel Clamp Engagement
steps.instructions: Check that the barrel clamp fully captures the syringe barrel. Make sure the barrel clamp is not lifted, partially seated, obstructed, or held away from the syringe. Confirm the syringe size and position appear correct for the selected syringe type.

steps.title: Check Plunger Holder Engagement
steps.instructions: Confirm the plunger holders are properly engaged around the syringe plunger. Make sure the plunger driver is not misaligned, forced, or partially engaged. Correct any obvious syringe loading issue before retesting the alarm.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Look for cracked plastic, bent holders, sticky residue, dried medication, broken clips, or debris near the barrel clamp, flange holder, and plunger holder area. Clean external surfaces according to facility procedure if contamination is present. Do not open the pump or perform internal disassembly during front-line troubleshooting.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Compatible Syringe
steps.instructions: Load a known-good syringe of the correct type and size. Confirm the selected syringe model and size match the syringe being used. If the alarm clears with a known-good syringe, suspect syringe compatibility, syringe damage, or loading technique.

steps.title: Check the Flange Sensor in Biomed Diagnostics
steps.instructions: Access Biomed > Diagnostics > Monitor Digital Sensors if available to trained service personnel. Observe the flange sensor state while moving the flange holder through its normal range. The flange sensor state should change appropriately as the flange holder is moved.

steps.title: Compare Sensor Behavior to Mechanical Movement
steps.instructions: While watching the digital sensor state, slowly move the flange holder and confirm the sensor response matches the physical movement. If the sensor does not change, changes intermittently, or shows the wrong state, suspect a flange holder, sensor, wiring, or related electronics issue. If the mechanical movement is sticky or incomplete, correct the external obstruction or remove the pump from service for evaluation.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after reloading the syringe, correcting holder position, or removing obstruction, the likely cause was syringe misloading or mechanical interference. If the alarm remains with a known-good syringe and the flange holder moves normally, the issue is likely device-related. Do not return the pump to clinical use if flange sensor response is inconsistent or cannot be verified.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 displaying Check Syringe Flange Sensor alarm. Pump would not accept syringe loading and infusion could not be started.
CCR.Cause: Syringe flange holder was not fully returning to the side of the case due to external residue near the holder path. Flange sensor remained active during syringe loading.
CCR.Resolution: Removed pump from service for evaluation. Cleaned external flange holder area per facility procedure, confirmed barrel clamp, flange holder, and plunger holders moved freely. Verified flange sensor changed state correctly in Biomed > Diagnostics > Monitor Digital Sensors. Reloaded known-good syringe and alarm cleared. Pump passed functional check and was returned to service.

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 Infusion Pump - Alarm: Check Syringe Plunger Sensor
description: Troubleshooting Check Syringe Plunger Sensor alarms caused by syringe loading, plunger holder position, startup movement, or plunger sensor detection issues that may affect syringe engagement confidence.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the pump from patient use if the alarm affects delivery. Transfer therapy to another verified pump if infusion must continue. Do not continue using the pump if syringe position or plunger engagement cannot be trusted.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Check Syringe Plunger Sensor. Observe whether the alarm occurs at startup, after loading a syringe, when the plunger driver is adjusted, during infusion setup, or intermittently during use.

steps.title: Check Whether the Syringe Is Loaded Correctly
steps.instructions: Remove and reload the syringe carefully. Confirm the barrel, flange, plunger, and barrel clamp are fully seated and engaged. Make sure the syringe is not crooked, loose, or partially seated.

steps.title: Check the Syringe Plunger Holders
steps.instructions: Inspect the plunger holders for sticking, binding, damage, loose movement, contamination, medication residue, or obstruction. Confirm they open, close, and return freely.

steps.title: Confirm the Barrel Clamp and Flange Holder Are Not Contributing
steps.instructions: Check that the barrel clamp lifts and lowers smoothly, the flange holder accepts the syringe flange correctly, and there is no dried fluid, tape, debris, or physical damage affecting syringe loading.

steps.title: Restart the Pump Without Moving the Plunger Driver
steps.instructions: Power the pump off. Make sure the plunger driver is not pushed, pulled, or bumped during startup. Power the pump back on and observe whether the alarm returns.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Compatible Syringe
steps.instructions: Load a known-good syringe of the correct model and size approved for the pump configuration. Confirm the syringe is not damaged, warped, sticky, or missing clear plunger geometry.

steps.title: Check for User Technique or Setup Pattern
steps.instructions: Ask when the alarm appeared and look for patterns such as moving the plunger driver during startup, lifting the barrel clamp, partial loading, wrong syringe model or size, or loading the plunger before the flange is seated.

steps.title: Use Biomed Diagnostics to Check the Plunger Sensors
steps.instructions: In a controlled service setting, use Biomed > Diagnostics > Monitor Digital Sensors. Check both plunger sensors while moving the plunger holders and confirm each sensor changes state correctly.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Cause
steps.instructions: Use the results to separate the problem into syringe loading, startup handling, syringe compatibility, mechanical plunger holder movement, or device-related sensor detection failure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears and the pump passes functional setup checks with proper syringe loading, return the pump to service and document what was corrected.

CCR.Complaint: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 reported Check Syringe Plunger Sensor alarm during syringe setup. User stated pump would not proceed with syringe loaded.
CCR.Cause: Syringe plunger was not fully captured by the plunger holders. Barrel clamp, flange holder, and plunger holder engagement were checked. Both plunger sensors were verified in Biomed digital sensor diagnostics.
CCR.Resolution: Reloaded syringe correctly and confirmed plunger holders moved freely. Alarm cleared. Pump completed setup with known-good syringe and was returned to service.

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 Infusion Pump - Alarm: Syringe Flange Not In Place
description: Troubleshooting Syringe Flange Not In Place alarms caused by syringe loading, flange clip position, sensor obstruction, syringe compatibility, or flange sensor failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the pump from patient use if the alarm affects infusion delivery. Transfer the infusion to another verified pump if therapy must continue. Do not repeatedly open, reload, or manipulate the syringe while the pump is being relied on for active delivery.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Syringe Flange Not In Place. Note whether the alarm occurs during syringe loading, at infusion start, during delivery, or intermittently when the pump is touched or moved.

steps.title: Check Syringe Loading
steps.instructions: Remove and reload the syringe. Confirm the syringe barrel is seated properly and the syringe flange is fully inserted into the flange clip. Make sure the syringe ears are not sitting above, behind, or partially outside the flange holder.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Syringe Type and Size Are Being Used
steps.instructions: Confirm the syringe brand, model, and size match what is selected or expected by the pump configuration. Check for syringe damage, unusual flange shape, incorrect syringe style, or a syringe not approved for the configured pump setup.

steps.title: Inspect the Flange Clip and Syringe Holder Area
steps.instructions: Inspect the flange clip and holder area for visible damage, loose parts, cracked plastic, dried fluid, contamination, or obstruction. Confirm the syringe can sit fully into place.

steps.title: Check for User Handling or Loading Technique Issues
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff whether the alarm happens only with certain users, certain syringes, or after syringe changes. Review proper syringe loading technique if the syringe flange is not being fully seated.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Syringe of the Same Approved Type
steps.instructions: Load a known-good syringe that matches the pump configuration. Confirm the flange seats securely and the alarm clears.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Flange Detection
steps.instructions: With no patient connected, observe whether the alarm appears when the syringe is gently seated, removed, or repositioned. Do not force the syringe or flange clip.

steps.title: Use Biomed Diagnostics to Check the Flange Sensor
steps.instructions: Use Biomed > Diagnostics > Monitor Digital Sensors and observe the syringe flange sensor state while inserting and removing the syringe flange. The sensor status should change reliably.

steps.title: Isolate the Problem
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is caused by loading technique, syringe compatibility, flange clip damage, contamination, sensor instability, or flange sensor failure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: Return the pump to service only after the syringe flange is detected correctly, the alarm clears, and normal syringe recognition is verified during functional testing.

CCR.Complaint: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 reported Syringe Flange Not In Place alarm during syringe loading. User stated pump would not accept syringe setup and alarm returned after attempted reload.
CCR.Cause: Syringe flange was not fully seated in the flange clip. Flange sensor response verified in Biomed > Diagnostics > Monitor Digital Sensors after correct syringe placement.
CCR.Resolution: Reloaded syringe correctly, confirmed flange fully inserted into flange clip, verified alarm cleared, and confirmed flange sensor changed state properly in diagnostics. Pump passed functional check and was returned to service.

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 Infusion Pump - Alarm: Syringe Plunger Not In Place
description: Troubleshooting Syringe Plunger Not In Place alarms caused by syringe loading, plunger holder engagement, syringe compatibility, contamination, or plunger sensor detection issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the pump from patient use if the alarm prevents reliable infusion delivery. Transfer the infusion to another verified pump if therapy must continue.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Syringe Plunger Not In Place. Observe whether the alarm occurs during startup, syringe loading, infusion start, handling, or syringe adjustment.

steps.title: Check Syringe Loading First
steps.instructions: Remove and reload the syringe carefully. Confirm the syringe barrel is seated correctly and the syringe plunger is fully captured by both plunger holders.

steps.title: Confirm the Syringe Is Compatible and Properly Selected
steps.instructions: Verify the syringe brand, model, and size selected on the pump match the installed syringe. Check for damaged, warped, cracked, or unusual syringe components.

steps.title: Inspect the Plunger Holder Area
steps.instructions: Inspect the plunger holders for cracks, looseness, sticking, contamination, fluid residue, missing pieces, or uneven movement.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Contamination or Obstruction
steps.instructions: Look for medication residue, tape, labels, dirt, or dried fluid around the plunger holder and sensor area. Clean exterior surfaces according to approved facility and manufacturer guidance.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Syringe
steps.instructions: Load a known-good syringe of the same approved type and size. If the alarm clears, the original syringe or syringe setup was likely the cause.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Behavior
steps.instructions: With no patient connected, gently manipulate the syringe and plunger holder area while observing whether the alarm appears or clears.

steps.title: Use Biomed Diagnostics to Check Plunger Sensors
steps.instructions: Access Biomed diagnostics according to facility process and use Monitor Digital Sensors to check both plunger sensor states while moving the plunger holder mechanism.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Cause
steps.instructions: Use findings to determine whether the issue is syringe placement, syringe compatibility, plunger holder movement, sensor response, mechanical wear, or internal detection failure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears and the pump passes basic functional checks, return the device to service according to facility policy and document the corrective action.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Syringe Plunger Not In Place alarm on Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000. Pump would not start infusion because syringe plunger engagement was not detected.
CCR.Cause: Syringe plunger was not fully captured by the plunger holders. Alarm cleared after syringe was reloaded correctly and plunger holder engagement was verified.
CCR.Resolution: Reloaded syringe correctly, confirmed syringe plunger was secured by both plunger holders, verified alarm cleared, and performed basic pump setup check. Device returned to service.

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 Infusion Pump - Alarm: Invalid Syringe Size
description: Troubleshooting Invalid Syringe Size alarms on the Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 caused by syringe selection mismatch, improper loading, barrel clamp movement, configuration limits, or barrel clamp sensing issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the pump is connected to a patient and the alarm interrupts infusion, notify clinical staff immediately. Do not troubleshoot extensively while the patient is dependent on the affected pump. Transfer the infusion to another verified pump if therapy must continue.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the display shows Invalid Syringe Size. Determine whether the alarm occurs during syringe loading, programming, infusion start, active delivery, or after the barrel clamp was moved or lifted.

steps.title: Confirm the Syringe Model and Size Selection
steps.instructions: Review the programmed syringe manufacturer, model, and size. Compare the selected syringe to the physical syringe installed and correct any mismatch.

steps.title: Inspect Syringe Loading
steps.instructions: Reload the syringe correctly. Confirm the barrel is seated, the flange is positioned, the plunger is captured, the barrel clamp is lowered, and the syringe is not crooked, loose, or partially seated.

steps.title: Check for Barrel Clamp Movement During Delivery
steps.instructions: Ask whether the barrel clamp was lifted, bumped, or adjusted during infusion. Reload the syringe correctly and remind users not to lift the barrel clamp during active delivery.

steps.title: Inspect the Barrel Clamp Area
steps.instructions: Check the barrel clamp for cracks, loose movement, sticking, contamination, fluid residue, physical damage, or improper return to the closed position. Clean external contamination according to facility procedure.

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Syringe
steps.instructions: Use a known-good syringe of an approved model and size. Load it carefully and confirm whether the pump recognizes it without displaying Invalid Syringe Size.

steps.title: Check Configuration or Drug Library Limits
steps.instructions: Confirm the selected syringe size is allowed for the active care area, drug program, or pump configuration. Escalate to the PharmGuard Toolbox or pump configuration administrator if the selection appears invalid.

steps.title: Perform Barrel Clamp Calibration or PM Check if Sensor Function Is Questionable
steps.instructions: If the alarm remains after confirming syringe selection, loading, and technique, perform the PM check of barrel clamp calibration per facility procedure and verify sensor response.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If correcting syringe selection, reloading, replacing the syringe, correcting configuration, or verifying barrel clamp operation resolves the alarm, complete functional testing before returning the pump to service.

CCR.Complaint: User reported Medfusion 4000 alarming Invalid Syringe Size during syringe setup or delivery. Pump would not reliably recognize the installed syringe size.
CCR.Cause: Syringe selection and loading were checked. Alarm was associated with incorrect syringe selection, improper loading, barrel clamp movement during delivery, or possible barrel clamp sensing issue.
CCR.Resolution: Verified correct syringe model and size, reloaded syringe, checked barrel clamp position and condition, and confirmed alarm cleared during functional check. If alarm persisted, pump was removed from service for barrel clamp calibration check and bench evaluation.

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 Infusion Pump - Alarm: Motor Not Running
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Motor Not Running alarm on the Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 infusion pump where the motor stops, stalls, or cannot reliably drive syringe delivery.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the pump from patient use if the Motor Not Running alarm is active. Transfer therapy to another verified infusion pump if infusion must continue.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is Motor Not Running. Observe whether it occurs at startup, infusion start, during delivery, intermittently, or after syringe loading or plunger movement.

steps.title: Check AC Power and Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Connect the pump to a known-good AC outlet. Verify normal power behavior and check for low battery or depleted battery conditions.

steps.title: Inspect the Syringe Installation
steps.instructions: Confirm the syringe barrel, flange, plunger, and selected syringe size are correct. Inspect for cracked, wedged, damaged, or misaligned syringe components.

steps.title: Check for External Mechanical Resistance
steps.instructions: Inspect the plunger driver and syringe loading area for sticky residue, foreign material, cracked syringe pieces, obstruction, bent parts, or impact damage.

steps.title: Check for Drive Movement During a Controlled Test
steps.instructions: In a non-patient bench setup, observe whether the plunger driver moves smoothly when the pump attempts to run. Document no movement, stalling, jerky travel, abnormal sound, or immediate alarm recurrence.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Binding in Accessible Areas
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible drive and plunger movement areas for binding, rough travel, unusual resistance, grinding, clicking, loose movement, or uneven motion without performing deep disassembly.

steps.title: Review Related Motor or Plunger Alarms
steps.instructions: Document any related alarms such as Motor Rate Error, Plunger Sensor Failure, syringe position alarms, syringe size alarms, calibration messages, or force sensor-related alarms.

steps.title: Use Biomed Diagnostics if Available
steps.instructions: Use approved Biomed diagnostic functions to evaluate motor and drive behavior. Confirm whether commanded motor movement and sensor feedback are consistent.

steps.title: Differentiate External Setup Causes from Device Failure
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after correcting syringe loading, obstruction, or contamination, the likely cause was external. If it persists with proper setup, stable power, and multiple syringes, suspect internal motor, sensing, electrical, or drive-train failure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If corrected, perform an operational check confirming normal power, syringe loading, infusion start, smooth plunger movement, no returned motor alarms, and functional alarm indicators before return to service.

CCR.Complaint: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 removed from use for Motor Not Running alarm. Staff reported pump would not continue infusion and plunger movement could not be trusted during delivery.
CCR.Cause: Motor Not Running alarm confirmed during bench check. Syringe loading, AC power, battery status, and external obstruction were checked. Alarm persisted with proper setup, indicating likely internal motor sensing, motor assembly, or drive-train failure.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service and labeled Out of Service. Referred for repair evaluation of motor sensor, motor assembly, worm gear, worm coupling, motor bracket, and related drive-train components.

title: Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 Infusion Pump - Alarm: Motor Rate Error
description: Troubleshooting Motor Rate Error on the Smiths Medical Medfusion 4000 when motor speed mismatch, syringe resistance, sensing issues, or worn drive-train components may affect infusion delivery.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
